title.original: Drivers of Host-Pathogen Community Assemblages in European Forests and Urban Green Spaces.
title.translated_zh: Drivers of Host-Pathogen Community Assemblages in European Forests and Urban Green Spaces

abstract.original: Despite advances in understanding infectious diseases, the persistence and re-emergence of wildlife pathogens continue to raise public and veterinary health concerns. This study investigates the relationship between biodiversity and rodent-borne diseases in Europe, focusing on habitat alterations and their impact on rodent diversity. We present host-pathogen data from 21 temperate forest sites and eight urban green spaces throughout five European countries, environments where rodents are abundant and human/domestic animals-wildlife interactions are likely to occur. From 2020 to 2022, 3766 specimens comprising 15 different small mammal species were analyzed. Samples were screened for bacteria via 16S rRNA sequencing or PCR, and for viral antibodies using immunofluorescent assays. Pathogens from several genera, including Bartonella, Borrelia, Mycoplasma, Anaplasma, Neoehrlichia, Leptospira, Orthohantavirus, and Orthopoxvirus, were detected at non-negligible prevalence in 11 host species. Host community composition differed between habitats, with more urban adapters in parks than in forests. Pathogen richness increased with an increase in host species diversity, supporting the "host-diversity begets parasite-diversity" hypothesis, though not with anthropization. The absence of some vector-transmitted parasites in urban areas suggests a shift in pathogen community driven by human impact. Host species and intrinsic factors were dominant explanatory variables for Mycoplasma species and Sarcocystidae, while extrinsic environmental and climatic factors influenced variations in several vector-transmitted pathogens. Apodemus sylvaticus and Clethrionomys glareolus served as important connector hosts in urban spaces and temperate forests, respectively. These results improve our understanding of the complex local host-pathogen system, aiding future management decisions and supporting the public health sector.

From 2020 to 2022, 3766 specimens comprising 15 different small mammal species were analyzed.
Samples were screened for bacteria via 16S rRNA sequencing or PCR, and for viral antibodies using immunofluorescent assays.

Pathogens from several genera, including Bartonella, Borrelia, Mycoplasma, Anaplasma, Neoehrlichia, Leptospira, Orthohantavirus, and Orthopoxvirus, were detected at non-negligible prevalence in 11 host species.
Host community composition differed between habitats, with more urban adapters in parks than in forests.
Pathogen richness increased with an increase in host species diversity, supporting the "host-diversity begets parasite-diversity" hypothesis, though not with anthropization.
The absence of some vector-transmitted parasites in urban areas suggests a shift in pathogen community driven by human impact.

Host species and intrinsic factors were dominant explanatory variables for Mycoplasma species and Sarcocystidae, while extrinsic environmental and climatic factors influenced variations in several vector-transmitted pathogens.
Apodemus sylvaticus and Clethrionomys glareolus served as important connector hosts in urban spaces and temperate forests, respectively.
These results improve our understanding of the complex local host-pathogen system, aiding future management decisions and supporting the public health sector.

ai_analysis.translated_title: Drivers of Host-Pathogen Community Assemblages in European Forests and Urban Green Spaces
ai_analysis.one_sentence_takeaway: This study reveals that host species diversity increases pathogen richness in European forests and urban green spaces, with habitat type influencing host-pathogen community composition and pathogen presence.
ai_analysis.study: The study analyzed 3766 specimens from 15 small mammal species collected between 2020 and 2022 across 21 temperate forest sites and eight urban green spaces in five European countries. Samples were screened for bacterial pathogens using 16S rRNA sequencing or PCR and for viral antibodies via immunofluorescent assays. The research investigated the relationship between biodiversity, habitat alteration, and rodent-borne diseases, focusing on host-pathogen community assemblages and environmental factors influencing pathogen prevalence.
key_findings: Host community composition differs between forest and urban habitats, with more urban adapter species in parks than in forests.
Pathogen richness increases with host species diversity, supporting the 'host-diversity begets parasite-diversity' hypothesis.
Anthropization (human impact) does not increase pathogen richness but shifts pathogen community composition, evidenced by the absence of some vector-transmitted parasites in urban areas.
Host species and intrinsic factors primarily explain the presence of Mycoplasma species and Sarcocystidae, while extrinsic environmental and climatic factors influence vector-transmitted pathogens.
Apodemus sylvaticus and Clethrionomys glareolus act as important connector hosts in urban and forest habitats, respectively.
quantitative_results: 3766 specimens analyzed from 15 small mammal species
Data collected from 21 temperate forest sites and 8 urban green spaces across 5 European countries
ai_analysis.significance: The findings enhance understanding of local host-pathogen dynamics in different habitats, informing future management strategies and supporting public health efforts related to wildlife-borne diseases.
ai_analysis.evidence_strength: The study is based on a large sample size collected over multiple years and sites, using molecular and serological methods to detect a range of pathogens, providing robust data on host-pathogen assemblages. However, only the abstract is available, so detailed methodological limitations or statistical analyses are not reported.
uncertainties: Specific statistical measures and confidence intervals are not available due to abstract-only data.
Details on pathogen prevalence rates and their variation across sites are not provided.
Potential limitations or biases in sampling and detection methods are not reported.

title.original: Hantavirus on a Cruise Ship: What COVID-19 Taught Us.
title.translated_zh: Hantavirus on a Cruise Ship: What COVID-19 Taught Us.

abstract.original: Delayed recognition of the mechanics of aerosol transmission - the primary mode of spread of SARS-CoV-2 - at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic led to inadequate early recommendations regarding nonpharmaceutical interventions and poor initial messaging about how to reduce infection risk. We highlight key epidemiological details of Andes virus (ANDV) transmission on the MV Hondius cruise ship and during prior reported outbreaks with confirmed person-to-person spread in Argentina, which, together, suggest aerosol spread may be a key mechanism of transmission of ANDV between source cases and susceptible contacts in permissible environmental settings.

sentences.text: Delayed recognition of the mechanics of aerosol transmission - the primary mode of spread of SARS-CoV-2 - at the beginning of the COVID-19 pandemic led to inadequate early recommendations regarding nonpharmaceutical interventions and poor initial messaging about how to reduce infection risk.

We highlight key epidemiological details of Andes virus (ANDV) transmission on the MV Hondius cruise ship and during prior reported outbreaks with confirmed person-to-person spread in Argentina, which, together, suggest aerosol spread may be a key mechanism of transmission of ANDV between source cases and susceptible contacts in permissible environmental settings.

bibliography.journal: Clinical infectious diseases : an official publication of the Infectious Diseases Society of America

ai_analysis.translated_title: Hantavirus on a Cruise Ship: What COVID-19 Taught Us.
ai_analysis.one_sentence_takeaway: The study suggests that aerosol transmission may be a key mechanism for Andes virus spread in certain environments, informed by epidemiological details from a cruise ship outbreak and prior cases in Argentina, highlighting lessons learned from COVID-19 about airborne transmission.

study.setting: MV Hondius cruise ship and prior outbreaks in Argentina

key_findings: Delayed recognition of aerosol transmission of SARS-CoV-2 led to inadequate early recommendations and poor messaging on infection risk reduction (A1).

Epidemiological details from the MV Hondius cruise ship outbreak and prior outbreaks in Argentina suggest aerosol spread may be a key transmission mechanism for Andes virus between source cases and susceptible contacts in certain environmental settings (A2).
ai_analysis.significance: The findings emphasize the importance of recognizing aerosol transmission in managing infectious diseases, using insights from COVID-19 to better understand and control Andes virus spread in specific environments.
ai_analysis.limitations: Only an abstract is available; detailed study design, sample size, quantitative data, and comprehensive results are not provided.
ai_analysis.evidence_strength: Limited to abstract-level information without peer-reviewed full text or detailed data.
uncertainties: Lack of detailed methodology and quantitative data limits assessment of the strength of evidence.
Unclear if findings are based on observational data, modeling, or other study types.
No information on interventions or outcomes beyond transmission hypotheses.

title.original: Preparing for the Next Pandemic: Learning From COVID-19 to Build What Comes Next.
title.translated_zh: Preparándose para la próxima pandemia: aprendiendo del COVID-19 para construir lo que viene después.

abstract.original: WHO's efforts to strengthen pandemic preparedness-grounded in what the world learned during COVID-19 and what today's outbreaks of avian influenza, Hantavirus and Ebola are teaching us.

sentences.text: WHO's efforts to strengthen pandemic preparedness-grounded in what the world learned during COVID-19 and what today's outbreaks of avian influenza, Hantavirus and Ebola are teaching us.

ai_analysis.translated_title: Preparándose para la próxima pandemia: aprendiendo del COVID-19 para construir lo que viene después.
ai_analysis.one_sentence_takeaway: El artículo destaca los esfuerzos de la OMS para fortalecer la preparación ante pandemias basándose en las lecciones aprendidas durante el COVID-19 y los brotes actuales de influenza aviar, hantavirus y ébola.
key_findings: La OMS está fortaleciendo la preparación para pandemias basándose en las lecciones aprendidas durante la pandemia de COVID-19 y los brotes actuales de otras enfermedades infecciosas.
ai_analysis.significance: El documento subraya la importancia de aplicar las experiencias del COVID-19 para mejorar la respuesta global ante futuras pandemias.
ai_analysis.limitations: No se dispone de información detallada sobre limitaciones del estudio debido a que solo se proporciona el resumen.
ai_analysis.evidence_strength: Limitada, dado que solo se cuenta con el resumen y no el texto completo del artículo.
uncertainties: Falta de detalles sobre metodología, resultados específicos y limitaciones del estudio debido a la ausencia del texto completo.

title.original: Watching the Ship, Missing the Forest: Why Ecological Spillover, Reservoir Ecology, and Environmental Change Remain the True Hantavirus Threats in North America.
Hantavirus infections in a changing world: epidemiology, pathogenesis, and one health challenges.

abstract.original: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics. Transmitted primarily through rodent reservoirs, hantaviruses cause two major clinical syndromes: haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS). Despite being historically confined to specific geographic regions, recent environmental changes, increased human animal interaction, and global mobility have contributed to evolving epidemiological patterns. This review summarises current knowledge on hantavirus transmission, global epidemiology, pathophysiological mechanisms, clinical manifestations, and therapeutic approaches. Particular attention is given to the emerging global epidemiology of Seoul virus (SEOV), an urban-associated hantavirus linked to brown rats and pet rodents. In addition, it explores emerging challenges related to vaccine development, surveillance, and the integration of One Health strategies in the prevention and control of hantavirus infections. Strengthening multidisciplinary approaches and improving early detection remain critical to mitigating the global impact of these infections.

sentences.text: Hantavirus infections are zoonotic diseases of global relevance, characterised by significant morbidity and mortality and complex ecological dynamics.
Transmitted primarily through rodent reservoirs, hantaviruses cause two major clinical syndromes: haemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) and hantavirus cardiopulmonary syndrome (HCPS).

Despite being historically confined to specific geographic regions, recent environmental changes, increased human animal interaction, and global mobility have contributed to evolving epidemiological patterns.
This review summarises current knowledge on hantavirus transmission, global epidemiology, pathophysiological mechanisms, clinical manifestations, and therapeutic approaches.
Particular attention is given to the emerging global epidemiology of Seoul virus (SEOV), an urban-associated hantavirus linked to brown rats and pet rodents.
In addition, it explores emerging challenges related to vaccine development, surveillance, and the integration of One Health strategies in the prevention and control of hantavirus infections.
Strengthening multidisciplinary approaches and improving early detection remain critical to mitigating the global impact of these infections.

title.original: Serologic Evidence of Hantavirus Exposure in Humans, Italy, 2004-2018.
title.translated_zh: Evidencia serológica de exposición a hantavirus en humanos, Italia, 2004-2018.

abstract.original: Rodentborne hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV) circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed. We tested samples collected from 371 patients during 2004-2018 and detected LCMV IgG in 2.7% of samples and hantavirus IgG in 7.3%. Puumala virus-neutralizing antibodies were confirmed in 10 cases, indicating unrecognized local hantavirus circulation.

sentences.text: Rodentborne hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCMV) circulate in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed.
We tested samples collected from 371 patients during 2004-2018 and detected LCMV IgG in 2.7% of samples and hantavirus IgG in 7.3%.
Puumala virus-neutralizing antibodies were confirmed in 10 cases, indicating unrecognized local hantavirus circulation.

ai_analysis.translated_title: Evidencia serológica de exposición a hantavirus en humanos, Italia, 2004-2018.
ai_analysis.one_sentence_takeaway: Serologic testing of patients in northern Italy from 2004 to 2018 revealed the presence of antibodies against hantaviruses and lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus, indicating unrecognized local circulation of these viruses.
key_findings: LCMV IgG antibodies were detected in 2.7% of patient samples.
Hantavirus IgG antibodies were detected in 7.3% of patient samples.
Puumala virus-neutralizing antibodies were confirmed in 10 cases, indicating unrecognized local hantavirus circulation.
ai_analysis.significance: The detection of hantavirus and LCMV antibodies in patients suggests that these viruses circulate locally in northern Italy but are rarely diagnosed, highlighting a potential under-recognition of these infections.
ai_analysis.evidence_strength: Moderate; based on serologic testing of patient samples over a 14-year period, but full study design details and peer review status are not provided.
uncertainties: The study abstract does not specify the study design or sampling methods.
No information on clinical presentation or outcomes of the patients tested.
The extent of local hantavirus circulation beyond the detected cases is unknown.

title.original: Hantavirus on a Nature Cruise and the Risk of a Pandemic.
The 2026 Andes virus outbreak: What genomic stability reveals about virulence.
First detection and genetic characterisation of Porcine Circovirus 2 from wild Rattus species in Malaysia
title.translated_zh: First detection and genetic characterization of Porcine Circovirus 2 from wild Rattus species in Malaysia

abstract.original: Porcine circovirus 2 (PCV2) is a major swine pathogen, and recent findings suggest a broader cross-species transmission potential for circoviruses. In a pathogen surveillance program in Selangor, Malaysia, 28 rats and 18 treeshrews were screened. While all samples tested negative for hantavirus, molecular screening unexpectedly detected PCV2 in one wild Rattus norvegicus . Phylogenetic and sequence analyses confirmed the virus belongs to the predominant PCV2d genotype, designated as strain Siew/Rat/Feces/2026. This study reports the first detection of PCV2 in a Rattus species in Malaysia. The detection implies that wild rats might play a role in the environmental circulation of PCV2d in Malaysia, highlighting the importance of continuous surveillance to monitor its ecological distribution and zoonotic potential.

sentences.text: Porcine circovirus 2 (PCV2) is a major swine pathogen, and recent findings suggest a broader cross-species transmission potential for circoviruses.
In a pathogen surveillance program in Selangor, Malaysia, 28 rats and 18 treeshrews were screened.
While all samples tested negative for hantavirus, molecular screening unexpectedly detected PCV2 in one wild <italic>Rattus norvegicus</italic> .
Phylogenetic and sequence analyses confirmed the virus belongs to the predominant PCV2d genotype, designated as strain Siew/Rat/Feces/2026.
This study reports the first detection of PCV2 in a <italic>Rattus</italic> species in Malaysia.

The detection implies that wild rats might play a role in the environmental circulation of PCV2d in Malaysia, highlighting the importance of continuous surveillance to monitor its ecological distribution and zoonotic potential.

ai_analysis.translated_title: First detection and genetic characterization of Porcine Circovirus 2 from wild Rattus species in Malaysia
ai_analysis.one_sentence_takeaway: This study reports the first detection of Porcine Circovirus 2 (PCV2) in a wild Rattus species in Malaysia, suggesting wild rats may contribute to the environmental circulation of PCV2d.
key_findings: All samples tested negative for hantavirus.
PCV2 was detected in one wild Rattus norvegicus.
The detected virus belongs to the predominant PCV2d genotype.
This is the first report of PCV2 detection in a Rattus species in Malaysia.
quantitative_results: PCV2 detected in 1 wild Rattus norvegicus.
ai_analysis.significance: The detection of PCV2 in wild rats implies they might play a role in the environmental circulation of PCV2d in Malaysia, underscoring the importance of continuous surveillance to monitor its ecological distribution and zoonotic potential.
ai_analysis.evidence_strength: The evidence is based on molecular screening and phylogenetic analyses from a surveillance program; however, only one positive detection was reported among the sampled animals.
uncertainties: The broader ecological role of wild rats in PCV2 transmission remains to be fully elucidated.
Zoonotic potential of PCV2 in wild rats is suggested but not confirmed.

title.original: Hantavirus - Once Again in the News.
The Orthohantavirus G<sub>N</sub>/G<sub>C</sub> Spikes: Molecular Determinants of Infection and Targets for Antiviral Immune Interventions.

abstract.original: Orthohantaviruses are globally distributed, rodent-borne RNA viruses associated with severe and often lethal disease in humans. The documented person-to-person transmission of Andes virus, endemic in South America, underscores their pandemic potential, and yet no effective treatments are available, while vaccines exist only for Hantaan and Seoul viruses. Key targets for antiviral intervention are the tetrameric G<sub>N</sub>/G<sub>C</sub> spikes, which are displayed on the viral surface. Here we review current molecular and cellular insights into these spikes, focusing on their roles during the viral infection cycle, such as receptor-mediated cell entry, membrane fusion, viral assembly, and cell exit. We also examine recent advances in therapeutic antibody developments and identify structurally conserved immune hotspots linked with their possible inhibitory mechanism. Finally, we discuss progress in G<sub>N</sub>/G<sub>C</sub>-based vaccine research and clinical trials, highlighting key achievements and remaining challenges to overcome the public health threat posed by orthohantaviruses.

sentences.text: Orthohantaviruses are globally distributed, rodent-borne RNA viruses associated with severe and often lethal disease in humans.

The documented person-to-person transmission of Andes virus, endemic in South America, underscores their pandemic potential, and yet no effective treatments are available, while vaccines exist only for Hantaan and Seoul viruses.
Key targets for antiviral intervention are the tetrameric G<sub>N</sub>/G<sub>C</sub> spikes, which are displayed on the viral surface.

Here we review current molecular and cellular insights into these spikes, focusing on their roles during the viral infection cycle, such as receptor-mediated cell entry, membrane fusion, viral assembly, and cell exit.
We also examine recent advances in therapeutic antibody developments and identify structurally conserved immune hotspots linked with their possible inhibitory mechanism.

Finally, we discuss progress in G<sub>N</sub>/G<sub>C</sub>-based vaccine research and clinical trials, highlighting key achievements and remaining challenges to overcome the public health threat posed by orthohantaviruses.

title: The Orthohantavirus GN/GC Spikes: Molecular Determinants of Infection and Targets for Antiviral Immune Interventions.
The Orthohantavirus G<sub>N</sub>/G<sub>C</sub> Spikes: Molecular Determinants of Infection and Targets for Antiviral Immune Interventions.

title.original: Distribution and richness of Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts: Implications for zoonotic risk in Southern South America.

abstract.original: Orthohantaviruses represent a major zoonotic threat in Southern South America (SSA), where 13 native rodent species are recognized reservoir hosts of 15 different viral genotypes. The information on their geographic distribution remains scattered, limiting regional assessments of exposure risk. We compiled and curated occurrence records for the Orthohantavirus reservoir hosts in SSA and used ecological niche modeling to estimate their potential distributions. We integrated binary presence predictions to map reservoir and genotype richness as well as reservoir assemblages. The richness maps were combined with gridded human population data to estimate the number of people potentially exposed. Most Orthohantavirus reservoir species in SSA are distributed over eastern and northeastern Argentina, parts of Paraguay, Uruguay, and southern Brazil. Hotspots of reservoir richness were concentrated in the Humid Chaco, while the area of high genotype richness extends across the Chaco-Pampean plain. Exposure to a high diversity of pathogenic genotypes (≥7) was estimated to exceed 13 million people under the most precautionary scenario. This study provides the first integrated, region-wide assessment of Orthohantavirus reservoir distributions and richness in SSA, offering an updated ecological baseline to support surveillance, risk mapping, and prevention strategies.
